name: Helmet.js
what_it_does: Express.js middleware that sets security-related HTTP response headers. Helmet configures Content-Security-Policy, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, and a dozen other security headers with sensible defaults. Prevents common web security vulnerabilities (clickjacking, MIME sniffing, XSS) with minimal configuration for Express-based agent backends.
use_cases: Secure Express.js agent API backends by adding security headers that prevent common web attacks
Implement Content Security Policy for agent web applications to prevent XSS attacks
Enable HSTS (Strict Transport Security) for agent HTTPS endpoints to prevent SSL stripping
Prevent clickjacking attacks on agent admin dashboards by setting X-Frame-Options
Disable browser features that can be exploited in agent web UIs with Permissions-Policy header
not_for: Non-Express frameworks — Helmet is Express-specific; use similar libraries for Fastify (fastify-helmet) or other frameworks
API-only services without browser clients — most Helmet headers are browser-specific
Full application security — Helmet handles HTTP headers only; not a complete security solution
best_when: You're building an Express.js agent web application and want security headers configured with one line of code.
avoid_when: You're using Fastify (use @fastify/helmet), or your agent serves only API clients (not browser UIs) where most headers have no effect.

auth.notes: Security middleware — sets HTTP security headers. Not an auth library.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Configuration errors are thrown at startup time with clear messages. Header value errors include which header and why the value is invalid.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Middleware runs deterministically on each request.
known_agent_gotchas: Default CSP blocks inline scripts and eval — agent UIs using inline JavaScript or script injection will break immediately after adding Helmet
Helmet 5+ changed default behavior — upgrading from Helmet 4.x to 5.x requires reviewing changed CSP defaults
HSTS preloading is permanent — once set and submitted to browser preload lists, removing HSTS can cause site access issues for months
frame-ancestors in CSP conflicts with X-Frame-Options — when both are set, browser uses CSP; Helmet sets both by default
agent APIs serving both browsers and API clients should use different CSP policies — browser clients need stricter CSP than API-only endpoints
Helmet middleware position matters — placing Helmet after other middleware that sets headers may cause conflicts; add app.use(helmet()) early

security_score_components.security_notes: Security-focused library that improves OWASP security posture. Core security protection — should be on every Express app. No credentials or external calls.
